posts.title: The Future of Industrial Automation in 2026
posts.excerpt: Cobots, edge AI and digital twins are reshaping the factory floor. Here's what actually matters — and the hype to ignore.
body: Every January the automation world rediscovers the same buzzwords. The difference in 2026 is that several of them have finally crossed from conference keynote to genuine, deployable ROI. Here's our pragmatic read on what's worth your budget.
## 1. Collaborative robots have grown up

Cobots used to be a compromise: safe, but slow and limited. The latest generation closes much of that gap. Built-in force-torque sensing, no-code programming and better vision mean a single operator can redeploy a cobot across multiple tasks in an afternoon.
- **Machine tending** — loading and unloading CNC or moulding machines.
- **Light assembly** — screw-driving, pick-and-place and inspection hand-offs.
- **End-of-line** — palletising and packaging at human-scale throughput.
## 2. Edge AI moved from demo to default
Running inference at the edge drops latency, shrinks cloud bills, and keeps the line running even when the network does not. The best deployments do one narrow thing extremely reliably.
> A model that catches one defect class at 99.8% beats a general model that catches everything at 80%.
## 3. Digital twins, finally useful

The useful version is humble: a live, data-backed model of a line you can simulate against before making changes. It de-risks commissioning and lets you test control logic offline — no million-dollar platform required.
## 4. Predictive maintenance pays for itself

This is the trend with the clearest payback. Vibration, current and temperature signatures plus simple models routinely predict failures days ahead, turning unplanned downtime into scheduled service. We have seen ROI inside eight months on avoided downtime alone.
## The throughline

Instrument well, automate the narrow thing reliably, and keep humans in the loop where judgement matters. The companies pulling ahead are compounding small, dependable wins — not chasing the flashiest tech.

posts.title: Production-Ready ESP32 Firmware: A Field Guide
posts.excerpt: From watchdogs to safe configuration — the patterns that keep embedded fleets alive in the field.
body: A demo that works on your desk and a product that survives two years in the field are very different things. The gap is filled with unglamorous engineering. Here are the patterns we apply before any ESP32 product ships at scale.
## 1. Assume it will crash — and recover gracefully
Field devices reset. Enable the hardware watchdog, feed it from your main loop, and make boot idempotent.
```cpp
esp_task_wdt_init(&(esp_task_wdt_config_t){ .timeout_ms = 8000, .trigger_panic = true });
esp_task_wdt_add(NULL);
void loop() {
esp_task_wdt_reset();   // feed it every cycle
do_work();
Log the reset reason on every boot — patterns there tell you more about field reliability than any bench test.
## 2. Separate configuration from firmware
Keep calibration, thresholds and network settings in non-volatile storage (NVS), versioned and validated, so behaviour can change without reflashing.
- **Namespaced NVS** — group settings and version the schema.
- **Validate on load** — range-check every value and fall back to safe defaults.
- **Persist calibration** — store per-unit calibration once, at end of line.
> The fastest field fix is the one that needs no new firmware at all.
## 3. Design for power from the first line
Battery products live or die on average current. Sleep aggressively, batch radio activity, and measure real current with a power profiler — never trust the datasheet typical.
## 4. Make connectivity resilient
Reconnect with exponential backoff, buffer data while offline, and never block the main loop on the radio. Set sensible keep-alives and a last-will message.
## 5. Build in observability
Ship structured logs and periodic health beacons — uptime, free heap, RSSI, reset reason. Watching free heap over days is the cheapest leak detector there is.
## The mindset
Assume failure, make recovery automatic, and instrument everything. Do that, and a fleet of thousands becomes something you can sleep through the night with.

posts.title: Building Reliable Industrial IoT Sensor Networks
posts.excerpt: Topology, protocols and security choices that make IIoT deployments dependable at scale.
body: An IIoT deployment is only as valuable as the data you can trust. Get the foundations right and the dashboards take care of themselves.
## Start with the topology
- **Star** — one gateway, simple and cheap. Great for a single hall.
- **Mesh** — self-healing, redundant paths. Worth it across large or RF-hostile areas.
- **Hybrid** — star clusters bridged to a backbone. The realistic answer for most plants.
Survey before you commit — steel, motors and concrete all eat RF.
## Pick protocols on purpose
- **MQTT** — lightweight pub/sub; the default for cloud-bound telemetry.
- **OPC UA** — rich, modelled data and tight PLC integration.
- **LoRaWAN** — long range, low power, for sparse outdoor sensors.
- **Wired (Modbus/Ethernet)** — boring, deterministic, often the right call near the machine.
> Wireless is convenient, not free. If a sensor is bolted next to a PLC anyway, a wire is usually more reliable.
## Choose sensors for the failure mode
- **Vibration** — bearing and imbalance faults in rotating equipment.
- **Current signature** — motor health without touching the mechanics.
- **Temperature** — RTD for accuracy, thermocouple for range.
## Design the pipeline as a whole
Collect at the edge with buffering, filter locally, transmit compressed and encrypted, store in a time-series database, and alert with thresholds operators actually trust.
## Security is non-negotiable
TLS everywhere, per-device certificates, OT/IT segmentation, and updatable firmware. A flat network with one shared password is a breach waiting to happen.
## Pilot, then scale
Deploy a small, fully-instrumented pilot first. Validate signal quality, battery life and data integrity against reality — then roll out with confidence.
